askNext: What exact meaning of Multi-agent protocol is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
multi-agent-protocol.body: Multi-agent protocol belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
multi-agent-protocol.example: "The team said multi-agent protocol as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
multi-agent-protocol.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
multi-agent-protocol.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

multi-agent-protocol.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
multi-agent-protocol.whyExists: It exists because conversations around Agent2Agent need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: Which modalities can the system actually handle in production?
Does the model merely accept multiple formats, or does it use them jointly to improve the outcome?
How has performance been evaluated across the different input types?
multimodal.body: The adjective matters less than the task. The real question is whether the extra modality materially improves the outcome or simply decorates the feature list.
multimodal.example: "The feature was multimodal in the useful sense: it could read the chart, listen to the spoken explanation, and answer questions about the mismatch."
multimodal.misuse: It gets waved around as a prestige adjective even when a product merely accepts an image upload and produces a caption. True multimodal capability is not just accepting multiple inputs. It is being able to use them meaningfully for the task at hand.
multimodal.note: Multimodal can describe both input and output capabilities. A system that reads images and writes text is multimodal, even if it does not generate images in return.
multimodal.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model or stack that can ingest several types of data, map them into compatible internal representations, and produce outputs that reflect those combined signals. The hard part is often not modality support itself but maintaining reliability across them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system works across more than one type of data, with all the messy evaluation problems that implies.

multimodal.whyExists: Human tasks rarely arrive as clean text alone. Multimodal systems exist because many useful applications involve documents, screenshots, spoken language, visual context, or combinations of these.

askNext: How are images and pages chunked and captioned?
What is end-to-end latency with large media?
How do you cite non-text evidence for users?
multimodal-rag.body: Multimodal RAG expands the library. Libraries still need catalogues and librarians.
multimodal-rag.example: "Multimodal RAG retrieved the right diagram and the model still described last year's version."
multimodal-rag.misuse: Assuming a text RAG pipeline plus a vision model is enough. Chunking, alignment, OCR quality, and latency dominate outcomes.
multimodal-rag.practicalMeaning: Separate indexes per modality when needed, invest in parsing, and evaluate with multimodal questions, not text-only benchmarks.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Multimodal RAG retrieves and conditions generation on non-text or mixed modalities—images, audio, video, PDFs—using embeddings, captions, or specialised indexes alongside text retrieval.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Separate indexes per modality when needed, invest in parsing, and evaluate with multimodal questions, not text-only benchmarks.

multimodal-rag.whyExists: Enterprise knowledge is not only markdown. Products needed retrieval that matches how evidence actually exists.

askNext: Is this about interface design, product strategy, or model capability?
Does the vision depend on trust, habit, or lock-in?
Are we building a workflow tool, a companion, or a polite way to blur the line?
mustafa-suleyman.body: It is easier to mock the companion idea than to notice how many major products are inching toward it anyway.
mustafa-suleyman.example: "The roadmap had gone full Mustafa: less tool, more companion, with enough memory and polish to make dependence sound like convenience."
mustafa-suleyman.misuse: By treating "companion" language as either obviously profound or obviously absurd. It is better understood as a serious product thesis about persistence, personalisation, convenience, and software that wants to sit between the user and everything else.
mustafa-suleyman.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Mustafa Suleyman, they usually mean AI as a persistent product layer: more assistant, less query box; more relationship, less transaction.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A sign that the discussion is moving from model performance to user relationship design.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the thesis that AI should feel less like software you visit and more like software that stays with you.

mustafa-suleyman.whyExists: Because AI is not only a research contest. It is also a packaging contest, an interface contest, and a trust contest. Suleyman's name often marks the point where the conversation shifts from raw capability to how intelligence is turned into habit.
